# TODO

split css into core and style

+ [ ] flush deletes _calepin_cache/ and _calepin_files/ directories recursively but asks for confirmation. Also deletes latex compilation artefacts.
+ [ ] Convert src/structures/tabsets and friends to templates where possible
+ [ ] 404.qmd
+ [ ] Convert elements templates to Tera and simplify the dispatch
+ [ ] Blog post site template
+ [ ] Accessibility
+ [ ] Multi-format rendering: calepin basics.qmd -f html,latex,pdf
    - [ ] Validate that format names do not include commas
+ [ ] CSL helpers: https://github.com/citation-style-language/styles/
+ [ ] why is navbar_search in the .rs file rather than just the template.

## CSL

Searched the webSearched the webYes, hayagriva fully supports CSL. Here's a summary:
CSL support:

Hayagriva supports all styles provided in the official Citation Style Language repository — currently over 2,600. Rust
You can either supply your own CSL files or choose from about 100 bundled citation styles using the archive feature. GitHub

Architecture:

The Typst team built two Rust libraries for this: citationberg (a CSL 1.0 parser using serde) and hayagriva itself, which uses citationberg to implement CSL processing. Citationstyles

Usage in code involves loading a .csl file, parsing it with IndependentStyle::from_xml, and passing it to a BibliographyDriver. The docs have a full worked example for this.
Caveats: CSL compliance isn't 100% complete — there are some open issues around edge cases like ibid handling and certain position attributes not yet being fully spec-compliant, but for standard use it works well.

## YAML -> TOML compatibility layer

- [ ] Decide on a minimal set of supported Quarto preamble features
- [ ] Convert YAML to TOML for Quarto -> Calepin compatibility

## Math

+ [ ] Typst math is currently suppressed
+ [ ] LaTeX -> Typst math plugin

## Plugins

I want each format to be a separate plugin. That will force me to design a good plugin infrastructure for users.

## Engines

- [ ] docx should be more powerful and maybe not go through pandoc.
- [x] sh engine
- [x] Create an engines module to split code execution by language
- [x] Re-use key building blocks across languages languages
- [x] Python support
- [x] --preview

## Misc

- [ ] What is the point of _brand.yml if we already have YAML? Still support it, though.
- [ ] $ handling. Quarto does not require escape, IIRC.
    - Do NOT do this. I think we should stay strict and document it.
- [x] Table of contents

## Figures

- [ ] Default out-width for figures and diagrams (see tikz in diagrams.qmd in particular). Look at my golden ratio post.

## Execution

- [ ] How can an R tool add stuff to the preamble? Often useful in LaTeX.
- [ ] Code annotation (line markers `# <1>` with numbered explanations)
- [ ] Multiple figures from a single code chunk with `fig-subcap`
- [ ] Layout attributes on code chunks (`layout-ncol`, `layout`)
- [ ] Column option for code output placement (`column: margin`, `column: page`, etc.)

## Tables

- [ ] knitr_print() support
- [ ] Tables should be using a template and filter
- [ ] Advanced table features (Bootstrap classes, embedded markdown in cells)
- [ ] Subtables
- [ ] Table caption location: margin

## Websites

- [ ] built-in website template
- [ ] built-in website_minimal template
- [ ] built-in website_blog template
- [ ] countrycode
- [ ] tinytable
- [ ] modelsummary
- [ ] marginaleffects

## HTML

- [ ] Do not base64 by default?
- [ ] Embed resources

## Layout

- [ ] Column classes for content width (`.column-body`, `.column-page`, `.column-screen`, `.column-margin`)
- [ ] Outset variants (`.column-body-outset`, `.column-page-inset`)
- [ ] Left/right overflow (`.column-screen-left`, `.column-page-right`, etc.)
- [ ] Margin content (`.column-margin` for text, figures, tables)
- [ ] Margin references (`reference-location: margin`)
- [ ] Margin citations (`citation-location: margin`)
- [ ] Margin captions (`cap-location: margin`, `fig-cap-location: margin`)
- [x] Asides (`[text]{.aside}` → margin notes)
- [x] Landscape mode (`::: {.landscape}`)
- [ ] PDF page geometry options (`geometry:` front matter)
- [ ] Screen inset shaded (`column: screen-inset-shaded`)

## Conditional Content

- [x] `.content-visible` / `.content-hidden` with `when-format` / `unless-format`
- [x] `when-meta` / `unless-meta` (match against boolean metadata values with dot notation)

## Cross-References

- [x] Basic cross-refs: `@fig:`, `@sec:`, `@thm:` etc.
- [ ] Custom cross-reference types
- [x] Grouped references (`[@fig-one; @fig-two]` → "Figure 1; Figure 2")
- [ ] Code listing cross-references (`#lst-` prefix)
- [x] Equation numbering and cross-references (`$$ ... $$ {#eq-label}`, `@eq-label`)
- [ ] Callout cross-references
- [ ] Custom numbered float types

## Document

- [x] Numbered sections (`number-sections: true`)
- [x] Date handling (keywords `today`/`now`/`last-modified`)
- [x] Date format strings (`date-format: "%B %e, %Y"`)
- [ ] Language / localization (`lang:` option, custom translation strings)
- [x] File includes (`{{< include file.qmd >}}` shortcode)
- [x] Variables file (`_variables.yml` with `{{< var name >}}` shortcode)
- [x] Callout collapse/expand (`collapse=true`)
- [x] Callout appearance styles (`appearance: simple/minimal`)
- [x] Callout custom titles

## Scholarly Front Matter

- [x] Rich author metadata (affiliation, ORCID, email, roles, corresponding author)
- [x] Affiliation details (department, city, country, ROR/ISNI/Ringgold IDs)
- [x] Multiple authors with shared affiliations (`ref:` system)
- [x] Keywords
- [x] Copyright (holder, year, statement)
- [x] License (Creative Commons abbreviations, custom text, URL)
- [x] Citation metadata (`citation:` key for citeable articles)
- [x] Funding (awards, sources, investigator references)
- [x] Appendices

## Misc

- [ ] Performance profiling
- [ ] Architecture refactor
- [ ] DRY
- [ ] Simplification
- [ ] ANSI colors in txtfmt plugin
- [x] lorem plugin
- [ ] cache
- [ ] _calepin_cache/ directory for storing all the caches. Otherwise we get many folders. 
- [ ] _calepin_files/ directory for storing files. [should be customizable]
- [ ] _calepin_plugins/ directory for storing files
- [x] websites via plugin-based site builder
- [ ] websites with navbar
- [ ] better CSS
- [ ] Calepin always in italics with capital letter.
- [x] Calepin vs Quarto (docs/quarto.qmd)
- [x] Warn on dot-style option names (use dashes instead)
- [x] `-v` / `--version` flag
- [ ] Allow users to supply a postprocess.latex or postprocess.html that accepts the raw code and returns the same thing but modified. That is done automatically if in the _calepin directory
- [ ] How do lettered addpendices work?
- [x] `{.unnumbered}` / `{.unlisted}` sections
- [ ] Accessibility


## File paths

- [ ] Be super strict
- [ ] Be super consistent
- [ ] Be super simple
- [ ] Be cross platform
- [ ] Be clear about location of output and location of intputs
- [ ] Files paths are different in Typst, LaTeX, HTML, and Markdown

## Plugins

- [x] `lightbox` (postprocessor plugin)
- [x] `txtfmt`
- [x] `lorem` (shortcode plugin)
- [x] `astro` (site builder plugin)

## Docs

- [ ] HTML
    - [ ] Classless CSS
    - [ ] Standalone files by default

## Manual Code Review

- [x] `src/engines/`
- [ ] `src/filters/`
- [ ] `src/render/`
- [ ] `src/formats/`
- [ ] `src/structures/`
- [ ] `src/parse/`
- [ ] `src/` (root modules: main, types, plugins, cli, util, website, site_builder, preview, compile)


# TOML spec issues

## Stable template API is too large

The draft exposes a very big set of template variables and component-specific variables: title blocks, appendix blocks, author sub-components, theorem variants, brand variables, bibliography fragments, _lb and _rb, and more. That is generous, but it also turns internal rendering choices into a public ABI.

That creates two problems:

you will be afraid to refactor internals,
users will come to depend on variables that were only meant as conveniences.

I would define a smaller stable core:

Stable, guaranteed
body
format
meta
page
target
format_vars
toc
preamble
css
js
Best-effort / internal

Put everything else under something like:

internal.*
or computed.*
or mark it explicitly as experimental

Also, the brand-{name} variables are awkward. Hyphenated variable names are not a good Jinja-facing API. A dictionary is cleaner:

{{ brand.colors.primary }}
{{ brand.colors["primary-light"] }}
